text: The surgeon grasps the stomach and lifts it to access the lesser sac, revealing the anatomical space behind the stomach.
The surgeon carefully dissects through the greater omentum, a fatty layer hanging off the stomach, and proceeds to divide visible blood vessels within this area.
The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand to grasp and cauterize tissue, demonstrating precise control over the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument, passing bipolar energy between metal rings to cauterize tissue positioned between them.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer in the right hand, which employs bipolar energy to cauterize tissue. After sealing the blood vessels, a blade within the instrument divides the tissue.
The surgeon lifts the stomach to expose the pancreas located behind it, continuing the elevation to improve visibility of the underlying structures.

The surgeon divides the layer of fat, known as the greater omentum, hanging off the stomach, extending the dissection up to the diaphragm. The pancreas, a retroperitoneal organ located at the back of the abdomen, is being accessed.
The assistant pulls down on the adipose tissue to create tension, allowing the surgeon to divide the fat layer using surgical instruments.
The surgeon continues to dissect along the stomach, progressing towards the esophagus, using a vessel sealer to cauterize and divide tissue.
The surgeon exposes the spleen, visible as a purple organ in the background, while continuing the dissection around the stomach area.
The spleen is visible in the surgical field and is being prepared to be removed along with the specimen during the pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon prepares to remove the spleen along with the tail of the pancreas, ensuring to excise the surrounding lymph nodes due to their proximity to the tumor in the pancreatic tail.

The surgeon identifies the splenic artery and vein, which are attached to the posterior aspect of the pancreas. These vessels are carefully dissected and removed to facilitate the resection of the pancreatic tail, ensuring the spleen is taken out with the specimen to prevent ischemia.

The surgeon carefully dissects the connective tissue between the stomach and the superior pole of the spleen, ensuring separation to avoid injury to the stomach. The proximity of the stomach to the spleen is visible.

In the foreground, the splenic artery is visible, pulsing at the bottom right of the screen near the superior border of the pancreas. The surgeon lifts the stomach to create tension on the tissue, facilitating the division of attachments between the posterior side of the stomach and the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon continues to maneuver the stomach to fully expose the pancreas, preparing to define the edges of the pancreas once the stomach is moved aside.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the gastroepiploic blood vessels, which supply the greater curvature of the stomach, aiming to preserve them during the dissection.

The surgeon has completely mobilized the stomach and lifts it to expose the pancreas. The pancreas is visible, covered by a layer of fat, and is distinguishable by its slightly different texture and color compared to the surrounding fat.

The surgeon lifts the left gastric artery and vein, defining the superior border of the pancreas. Below the incision lies the pancreas, while the tissue above consists of fat containing lymph nodes targeted for removal in this cancer operation.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the left gastric artery and vein, ensuring these vessels remain intact as they supply blood to the stomach. The hepatic artery is identified, and a lymph node located over the hepatic artery is visually observed and removed to check for cancer spread.
The surgeon carefully dissects the tissue surrounding the hepatic artery, separating it from adjacent structures.

The surgeon traces the coronary vein towards its insertion on the portal vein. A lymph node adjacent to the vein is identified and removed, to be sent separately for pathological examination to check for cancer presence.

The surgeon provides a clear view of the pancreas, hepatic artery, and left gastric artery, and begins defining the inferior border of the pancreas. The pancreas is visually identified as a long, slender organ with distinct head, body, and tail sections. The surgeon prepares for a distal pancreatectomy by outlining the pancreas's edges, noting that the splenic artery and vein are located behind or beneath the pancreas, requiring elevation of the organ for access.
The surgeon exposes the fourth portion of the duodenum, visible below the surgical field, and identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) adjacent to the bowel.
The surgeon repositions the graspers to re-expose the pancreas, lifting it to reveal the underside where the splenic vein becomes visible.
The surgeon identifies the bluish-purple splenic artery beneath the pancreas, lifting the pancreas to expose the splenic vessels. The splenic artery is traced back and will need to be divided.
The surgeon removes lymphatic tissue and lymph nodes located above the pancreas, extending towards the diaphragm. The tumor within the pancreas is not visible during this part of the procedure.

The surgeon lifts the stomach using a retractor, revealing the left gastric vessels tented on the left side of the screen. Fatty tissue is being removed between the superior border of the pancreas and the left cruce of the diaphragm.

The surgeon identifies the splenic artery at its base and begins to separate surrounding lymph nodes and fatty tissue. The tissue is carefully dissected away from the artery, preparing it for division, and will be removed with the specimen.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery in the right hand to dissect and cauterize tissue around the pancreas, providing precise control over the surgical field.
The surgeon completes dissection around the origin of the splenic artery, preparing to place a clip on the artery before addressing the vein.
The surgeon places two clips on the side of the splenic artery that remains in the body, then uses scissors to cut the artery, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon prepares to address the vein located behind the pancreas, clearing space to position a stapler between the vein and the pancreas for division.
The previously divided splenic artery is visible, creating a clear space for the surgeon to pass a stapler between the pancreas and the vein.

The surgeon uses umbilical tape to lift the pancreas, facilitating the placement of a stapler. An ultrasound is performed to locate the tumor, confirming its position in the tail of the pancreas, to the right of the umbilical tape, ensuring safe stapler passage.

The surgeon uses a stapler equipped with a seam guard, a white cloth-like material secured by blue strings. After firing the stapler, the seam guard remains in place, requiring scissors to complete its division. The remaining visible structure is the vein, which is the last element to be addressed.
The surgeon uses hook cautery to detach the remaining attachments around the splenic vein. Once the vein is fully exposed, clips are placed on it before dividing the vein.

The surgeon places two clips on the side of the vein that will remain in the patient and one clip on the side with the specimen. After dividing the vein, they separate the veil of tissue behind the pancreas, following it to the spleen, and proceed to take down the attachments to the spleen, freeing the entire specimen.

The surgeon lifts the tail of the pancreas and uses a vessel sealer to bluntly dissect and divide the tissue behind the pancreas. The instrument is used to cauterize and separate the tissue planes, minimizing the risk of bleeding.

The surgeon lifts the freed pancreas and spleen, placing them into an endo-pouch. The spleen is positioned first, followed by the pancreas. The bag is closed, and a small incision is made to remove the bag containing the specimen, completing the robotic distal pancreatectomy.
